perturbation.original_text: The Distributor agrees to abide by Lucid's standard payment terms, which are as follows; One Hundred (100) per cent of order value by sixty ( 90 ) day irrevocable letter of credit when order is placed on Lucid.
perturbation.changed_text: The Distributor agrees to abide by Lucid's payment terms.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the specific payment terms eliminates consumer protections related to payment methods and timing. This violates laws requiring clear and conspicuous disclosure of payment terms, potentially exposing the distributor to unfair or deceptive practices claims.
perturbation.law_explanation: This law prohibits deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any business, trade or commerce or in the furnishing of any service in New York State. Omitting the payment terms obscures vital financial information, potentially misleading the distributor and contradicting the statute's requirement for transparent business practices.
perturbation.justification: The omission of specific payment terms, as described in the 'Changed Text', directly contradicts the principle of consumer protection from deceptive practices. The 'Perturbation Explanation' notes that removing these terms eliminates consumer protections and violates laws requiring clear disclosure of payment terms. The 'Law Explanation' further supports this by stating that omitting payment terms obscures vital financial information, misleading the distributor and contradicting requirements for transparent business practices. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references NYS General Business law, Article 22-A, concerning consumer protection from deceptive acts and practices, suggesting the omission could be construed as a deceptive practice by obscuring important financial information.

perturbation.original_text: Lucid will not have any liability or responsibility to Distributor or any other person or entity for any consequential, indirect, special, punitive or incidental damages or lost profits, whether foreseeable or unforeseeable, based on claims of Distributor or Distributor's customers (including but not limited to, claims for loss of data, goodwill, profits, use of money or use of product, interruption in use or availability of data stoppage or other work or impairment or assets) arising out of breach or failure of express or implied warranty, breach of contract, misrepresentation, negligence, strict liability in tort or otherwise, except only in the case of death or personal injury where and to the extent that applicable law requires such liability. In no event will the aggregate liability incurred by Lucid in any action or proceeding exceed the total amount actually paid to Lucid by Distributor for the purchase of the products that actually caused the damage or loss.
perturbation.changed_text: Lucid will not have any liability or responsibility to Distributor or any other person or entity for any consequential, indirect, special, punitive or incidental damages or lost profits, whether foreseeable or unforeseeable, based on claims of Distributor or Distributor's customers (including but not limited to, claims for loss of data, goodwill, profits, use of money or use of product, interruption in use or availability of data stoppage or other work or impairment or assets) arising out of breach or failure of express or implied warranty, breach of contract, misrepresentation, negligence, strict liability in tort or otherwise.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the exception for death or personal injury caused by negligence, the modified text violates the fundamental legal principle that one cannot contract away liability for gross negligence, recklessness, or intentional torts resulting in physical harm. This could be seen as an unconscionable contract provision.
perturbation.law_explanation: This New York law prohibits agreements exempting owners and contractors from liability for negligence in the construction, maintenance, and repair of buildings and other structures. While this law specifically relates to construction, the principle it embodies—that parties cannot contract away liability for their own negligence—is broadly applicable and removing the personal injury exception could be deemed void against public policy.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-322.1 Agreements exempting owners and contractors from liability for negligence void and unenforceable; certain cases General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 3 § 5-322.1. Agreements exempting owners and contractors from liability for negligence void and unenforceable; certain cases. 1. A covenant, promise, agreement or understanding in, or in connection with or collateral to a contract or agreement relative to the construction, alteration, repair or maintenance of a building, structure, appurtenances and appliances including moving, demolition and excavating connected therewith, purporting to indemnify or hold harmless the promisee against liability for damage arising out of bodily injury to persons or damage to property contributed to, caused by or resulting from the negligence of the promisee, his agents or employees, or indemnitee, whether such negligence be in whole or in part, is against public policy and is void and unenforceable; provided that this section shall not affect the validity of any insurance contract, workers' compensation agreement or other agreement issued by an admitted insurer. This subdivision shall not preclude a promisee requiring indemnification for damages arising out of bodily injury to persons or damage to property caused by or resulting from the negligence of a party other than the promisee, whether or not the promisor is partially negligent. 2. A covenant, promise, agreement or understanding in, or in connection with or collateral to a contract or agreement relative to the construction, alteration, repair or maintenance of a building, structure, appurtenances and appliances including moving, demolition and excavating connected therewith, purporting to condition a subcontractor's or materialman's right to file a claim and/or commence an action on a payment bond on exhaustion of another legal remedy is against public policy and is void and unenforceable; provided that this subdivision shall not affect the validity of any insu
perturbation.justification: The original contract excludes liability for certain damages, but explicitly makes an exception for death or personal injury where required by law. The modified contract removes this exception. The Perturbation Explanation indicates this removal could violate the principle that one cannot contract away liability for gross negligence resulting in physical harm, potentially making the provision unconscionable. The Law Explanation, while referencing construction-related law, suggests a broader principle against contracting away liability for one's own negligence. Scraped Law Snippet 1, though focused on construction, reinforces the concept that agreements exempting parties from liability for negligence are void and unenforceable. The omission of the exception for death or personal injury weakens the consumer's legal protection, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Distributor will supply to Lucid, on a monthly basis, a detailed listing of all sales prospects within the Territory. A sales prospect is considered by Lucid to be any End User who has expressed an interest in purchasing Lucid products.
perturbation.changed_text: The Distributor will supply to Lucid, on a monthly basis, a detailed listing of all sales prospects within the Territory.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the definition of "sales prospect" can lead to ambiguity and disputes about what information the distributor is obligated to provide. This omission could create a situation where consumer privacy is jeopardized if Lucid interprets 'sales prospects' too broadly, without a clear definition, thus violating data protection laws. The original definition provides a specific limitation to people who have expressed an interest in purchasing, without that limitation, the potential for violation of privacy laws is heightened.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York State Information Security Breach and Notification Act (General Business Law § 899-aa)
perturbation.law_explanation: This New York law requires businesses to protect the private information of residents. Removing the definition of 'sales prospect' creates ambiguity, potentially leading to the collection and transmission of data from individuals who have not expressed interest, thus increasing the risk of a data breach and violating privacy protections.
perturbation.justification: The omission of the 'sales prospect' definition weakens consumer privacy protections, potentially violating NYS General Business Law Article 39-F, which mandates data security protections. Without a clear definition, the distributor could collect data from individuals who haven't expressed interest, contradicting the law's intent to safeguard private information as indicated by the scraped snippet: 'SECTION 899-BB Data security protections'.
